source_paper_title: A Class of Dissimilarity Semimetrics for Preference Relations
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Nishimura_2024, title={A Class of Dissimilarity Semimetrics for Preference Relations}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0351}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0351}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Nishimura, Hiroki and Ok, Efe A.}, year={2024}, month=nov, pages={2249–2270} }
context:
Section 4, page 25 (end of Section 4, after Table 1)

In passing, we note that as an immediate consequence of Theorem 4.1, we have \(\mathrm{diam}_D(A(X)) \ge \mathrm{diam}_D(P(X)) \ge n2^{n-1} + 2 - 2^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} - 2^{\lceil n/2 \rceil}.\) We do not presently know whether or not either of these inequalities hold as equalities.
title: Determine diameter of acyclic and preorder preference spaces under top-difference semimetric
background_latex:
Let \(X\) be a finite set of alternatives with \(|X|=n\ge 2\). An (acyclic) preference relation in the paper is modeled as an \emph{acyclic order} \(\%\) on \(X\), i.e., a reflexive binary relation whose strict (asymmetric) part \(\succ\) contains no directed cycle: there do not exist pairwise distinct \(z_1,\dots,z_k\in X\) with \(z_1\succ z_2\succ\cdots\succ z_k\succ z_1\).

For any binary relation \(R\) on \(X\) and any nonempty subset \(S\subseteq X\), the set of \(R\)-maximal elements of \(S\) is
\[M(S,R):=\{x\in S: \text{there is no }y\in S\text{ with }y\,R^{>}\,x\},\]
where \(R^{>}\) denotes the asymmetric part of \(R\). For acyclic orders \(\%\), every nonempty finite \(S\) has \(M(S,\%)\neq\varnothing\).

Define the \emph{top-difference semimetric} \(D\) on the set \(A(X)\) of all acyclic orders on \(X\) by
\[D(\%,\mathcal{D}) := \sum_{S\subseteq X} \bigl| M(S,\%)\,\triangle\, M(S,\mathcal{D})\bigr|,\]
where \(\triangle\) is symmetric difference and \(|\cdot|\) is cardinality. (Singleton and empty \(S\) contribute \(0\), but the definition sums over all \(S\subseteq X\).)

For any subset \(\mathcal{C}\subseteq A(X)\), its diameter with respect to \(D\) is
\[\operatorname{diam}_D(\mathcal{C}) := \max\{ D(\%,\mathcal{D}) : \%,\mathcal{D}\in \mathcal{C}\},\]
which is well-defined since \(\mathcal{C}\) is finite when \(X\) is finite.

Let \(P(X)\subseteq A(X)\) denote the set of all preorders on \(X\) (reflexive and transitive relations).
problem_definition_latex:
Compute (or give an exact closed-form expression for)
\[\operatorname{diam}_D(A(X))\quad\text{and/or}\quad \operatorname{diam}_D(P(X))\]
as functions of \(n=|X|\), where \(D\) is the top-difference semimetric
\[D(\%,\mathcal{D}) := \sum_{S\subseteq X} \bigl| M(S,\%)\,\triangle\, M(S,\mathcal{D})\bigr|\]
on acyclic orders \(\%,\mathcal{D}\in A(X)\), and \(P(X)\) denotes the class of preorders on \(X\).

An (acyclic) preference relation in the paper is modeled as an \emph{acyclic order} \(\%\) on \(X\), i.e., a reflexive binary relation whose strict (asymmetric) part \(\succ\) contains no directed cycle: there do not exist pairwise distinct \(z_1,\dots,z_k\in X\) with \(z_1\succ z_2\succ\cdots\succ z_k\succ z_1\).

For any binary relation \(R\) on \(X\) and any nonempty subset \(S\subseteq X\), the set of \(R\)-maximal elements of \(S\) is

\[D(\%,\mathcal{D}) := \sum_{S\subseteq X} \bigl| M(S,\%)\,\triangle\, M(S,\mathcal{D})\bigr|,\]

Compute (or give an exact closed-form expression for)
\[\operatorname{diam}_D(A(X))\quad\text{and/or}\quad \operatorname{diam}_D(P(X))\]
as functions of \(n=|X|\), where \(D\) is the top-difference semimetric

on acyclic orders \(\%,\mathcal{D}\in A(X)\), and \(P(X)\) denotes the class of preorders on \(X\).
location_in_paper: Section 4, page 25 (end of Section 4, after Table 1)
open_problem_quote: In passing, we note that as an immediate consequence of Theorem 4.1, we have \(\mathrm{diam}_D(A(X)) \ge \mathrm{diam}_D(P(X)) \ge n2^{n-1} + 2 - 2^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} - 2^{\lceil n/2 \rceil}.\) We do not presently know whether or not either of these inequalities hold as equalities.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The quantities are finite maxima over finite (though huge) spaces; for small n one can brute-force or use MILP/DP to compute exact diameters and look for extremal pairs, providing data to refute conjectured equalities or suggest tight formulas.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate extremal constructions (pairs of relations) can be represented explicitly (e.g., adjacency matrices/DAGs), and the fitness signal is exactly computable via the polynomial-time formula for D given in the paper; evolutionary search can explore structures to maximize D for given n and hypothesize patterns.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In Nishimura–Ok (2022), the top-difference semimetric \(D(\%,\mathcal D)=\sum_{S\subseteq X}|M(S,\%)\triangle M(S,\mathcal D)|\) is introduced on the space \(A(X)\) of acyclic orders, motivated by comparing induced choice correspondences menu-by-menu. The paper provides axiomatic characterizations for \(D\) and its weighted variants \(D_\mu\), and derives alternative formulas (notably a polynomial-time computable expression) that avoid summing over all \(2^n\) subsets directly. In terms of global geometry, it computes the diameter exactly for complete preferences (total preorders) \(P_{\mathrm{total}}(X)\): \(\operatorname{diam}_D(P_{\mathrm{total}}(X)) = n2^{n-1}+2-2^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor}-2^{\lceil n/2\rceil}\), and also notes \(\operatorname{diam}_D(L(X))=2(2^n-n-1)\) for linear orders.

The open problem is to determine \(\operatorname{diam}_D(P(X))\) and/or \(\operatorname{diam}_D(A(X))\) as functions of \(n\). The source paper establishes only the lower bound \(\operatorname{diam}_D(A(X))\ge \operatorname{diam}_D(P(X))\ge \operatorname{diam}_D(P_{\mathrm{total}}(X))\) and explicitly states that it is unknown whether either inequality is an equality. Among the (few) forward citations provided here, one paper develops a weighted top-difference framework but restricts attention to linear orders, offering reformulations and axioms that could be useful for diameter computations on \(S_n\) yet does not address \(A(X)\) or \(P(X)\). Another citing paper is only tangential, referencing the metric viewpoint without contributing to diameter calculations.

At present, based on the available forward-citation evidence, the diameter of \((A(X),D)\) and \((P(X),D)\) remains open beyond the known exact formulas for \(L(X)\) and \(P_{\mathrm{total}}(X)\). Promising directions include constructing extremal pairs of acyclic orders/preorders that maximize menu-wise maximal-set disagreement, and leveraging the polynomial-time reformulation of \(D\) (in terms of principal ideals and overlap parameters) to turn the diameter problem into a combinatorial optimization over DAGs or transitive relations.

sources.title: On the Weighted Top-Difference Distance: Axioms, Aggregation, and Approximation
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper defines a family of weighted top-difference distances on linear orders (permutations) 
\[d^{\mu}_{\beta}(\sigma,\pi)=\sum_{S\subseteq[n],\ |S|\ge2}\beta_{|S|}\,\mu\bigl(M(S,\sigma)\triangle M(S,\pi)\bigr),\]
with computable reformulation 
\[d^{\mu}_{\beta}(\sigma,\pi)=\sum_{x\in[n]}\bigl(f_{\beta}(|x_{\downarrow,\pi}|)+f_{\beta}(|x_{\downarrow,\sigma}|)-2f_{\beta}(|x_{\downarrow,\sigma}\cap x_{\downarrow,\pi}|)\bigr)\mu(x),\]
where \(f_{\beta}(t)=\sum_{k=2}^n \beta_k\binom{t}{k-1}\). It also gives an axiomatic characterization (Theorem 9) of these distances among semimetrics on \(S_n\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the diameter under the (unweighted) top-difference semimetric \(D\) on the space of acyclic orders \(A(X)\) and/or preorders \(P(X)\). This paper studies a weighted generalization of the same top-difference construction but only on the restricted domain of linear orders \(S_n\) (rankings), not on all acyclic orders or preorders. It does not compute diameters of \((A(X),D)\) or \((P(X),D)\), nor even the diameter on \(S_n\) for the neutral unweighted case. Its main relevance is providing formulas and structural characterizations that could be used to attempt diameter computations in the linear-order subspace.

sources.title: Semantics meets attractiveness: Choice by salience
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper introduces and characterizes a context-dependent choice model (“rationalization by salience”, RS) and a testable special case (“rationalization by linear salience”, RLS). A central characterization (Theorem 3) states that for a choice function on a finite set, being RLS is equivalent to (ii) asymmetry of a revealed-salience relation \((\prec)\) defined from minimal WARP violations, and equivalent to (iii) absence of “conflicting menus”. The paper also proves existence of “moody” choices (Theorem 1) and that the fraction of moody choices tends to 1 as \(|X|\to\infty\) (Theorem 2).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the exact diameter of the space of acyclic orders (and of preorders) under the top-difference semimetric \(D(\%,\mathcal D)=\sum_{S\subseteq X}|M(S,\%)\triangle M(S,\mathcal D)|\). This citing paper does not study the semimetric \(D\), does not compute any diameters of \(A(X)\) or \(P(X)\), and does not analyze maximal-element symmetric-difference sums over all subsets. It only cites Nishimura–Ok (2022) in passing (as a possible “metric for preferences”) and develops a different framework about salience-based choice rationalizations. At most, it is tangentially related by pointing to the broader theme of defining meaningful distances/metrics on preference relations (footnote referencing ‘a semantically meaningful notion of metric for preferences’), but it provides no results usable to compute \(\operatorname{diam}_D(A(X))\) or \(\operatorname{diam}_D(P(X))\).
